strategies.description: A faster long-only trend-following rule than the 50/200 cross: holds a liquid quality name while its 20-DMA is above its 50-DMA and stands aside when the shorter average rolls under the longer one.
strategies.entry: Go long (paper) when the 20-DMA crosses above the 50-DMA and the daily close is above both averages; confirm the 20-DMA slope is turning up so the cross reflects genuine short-term momentum rather than a flat drift. Position on a positional (multi-week) horizon and size within the per-position rupee cap.
strategies.exit: Exit to cash when the 20-DMA crosses back below the 50-DMA, or when the daily close breaks decisively below the 50-DMA before the cross completes. Re-enter only on a fresh upward cross, accepting that this faster signal whipsaws more than the 50/200 pair.
filters: liquid large/mid-cap or index constituent
confirm 20-DMA slope turning up (reduce flat-chop whipsaw)
avoid names with structural governance red flags
turnover-aware: skip re-entry inside a narrow re-cross band
long-only, unlevered, size within per-position rupee risk
strategies.rationale: The 20/50 pair reacts to Indian equity trend changes roughly a quarter earlier than the 50/200 cross, capturing more of a leg while still filtering out day-to-day noise; the trade-off is higher turnover, which the cost-aware filters and low trade cap are designed to contain.

strategies.name: Dual Momentum Single-ETF Rotation
strategies.description: A single-holding rotation that combines absolute and relative momentum: it holds the stronger of a small set of broad-market ETF proxies only when that leader also has positive absolute momentum, otherwise it sits in a cash/liquid-ETF proxy.
strategies.entry: At each monthly review, rank a handful of liquid broad-market ETF proxies (e.g. large-cap index versus a diversified alternative) by their trailing 12-month total return (relative momentum). Buy (paper) the top-ranked ETF only if its own 12-month return also exceeds the risk-free / liquid-ETF proxy return (absolute momentum). Hold a single ETF at a time on a long-term cadence.
strategies.exit: At the next monthly review, rotate into the new leader if leadership changes, or move entirely to the cash/liquid-ETF proxy whenever the current holding's 12-month absolute momentum turns negative. Between reviews the position is held unchanged to keep turnover low.
filters: broad-market, high-liquidity ETF candidates only
monthly (not daily) rebalance to contain turnover and cost drag
low expense ratio / tight tracking on each candidate
cash/liquid-ETF proxy as the defensive out-of-market leg
long-only, unlevered, single holding at a time
strategies.rationale: Dual momentum stacks two robust effects documented across markets: relative momentum concentrates in the leading asset while absolute momentum steps aside during broad declines. For Indian long-term investors it offers a rules-based way to participate in trends yet defend capital in prolonged bear phases, at low, monthly-cadence turnover.

strategies.name: Absolute-Momentum Index-ETF Timing
strategies.description: A single-ETF time-series momentum rule that holds a broad-market index ETF only while its 12-month total return is positive and steps entirely to cash otherwise, aiming to sidestep sustained bear markets.
strategies.entry: At each month-end review, go long (paper) the broad-market index ETF when its trailing 12-month total return is positive (price above its level twelve months ago). Take the full intended allocation in one ETF and hold through the month.
strategies.exit: Move to cash (or a liquid-ETF cash proxy) at the month-end review whenever the trailing 12-month return turns non-positive; re-enter only when 12-month momentum flips positive again, keeping trades to at most one direction change per review.
filters: single broad-market, liquid index ETF
month-end evaluation only (avoid intramonth churn)
low expense ratio / tight tracking
cash proxy for the out-of-market state
long-only, unlevered
strategies.rationale: Time-series (absolute) momentum on a broad index is one of the most robust and least parameter-sensitive defensive rules known; the 12-month lookback historically keeps Indian investors invested through expansions and in cash through the worst of drawdowns, at very low turnover and fully transparent.

strategies.name: SIP Step-Up / Value-Averaging DCA
strategies.description: An executable accumulation baseline that automates buying so behaviour cannot sabotage compounding. It improves on a flat SIP by (a) stepping the contribution up with every salary increase, (b) value-averaging so more units are bought into weakness, and (c) optionally adding a lump-sum top-up rule on 10%+ index corrections. Rupee-cost averaging sizes contributions to the savings rate, not a market view.
strategies.entry: Contribute a fixed rupee amount into a broad-market index ETF on a set monthly date, sized to the savings rate rather than any market view. Each period apply (1) a step-up: raise the base contribution by a small fixed percentage annually to track rising income (salary increases); (2) a value-averaging adjustment: compute the target portfolio value on a smooth growth path and invest the amount needed to reach it, so the rupee contribution rises when price has fallen and eases when price has surged; and (3) an optional lump-sum top-up when the broad index draws down 10%+ from a recent high, deploying earmarked cash into the correction. Cap any single top-up at the base contribution multiplied by a modest bound.
strategies.exit: No tactical exit; this is an accumulate-and-hold baseline. If value-averaging implies the portfolio is far above its target path, the contribution is merely reduced (never a forced sale under the paper mandate). The long holding period is deliberate: Indian equity LTCG is taxed at 12.5% above Rs 1.25 lakh a year, whereas churning converts gains to 20% STCG plus costs. Redemption is modelled only for reporting horizons.
filters: broad-market, high-liquidity index ETF
low expense ratio / tight tracking
cap per-period top-up to bound cash-flow and cost drag
fixed contribution cadence with annual (salary-linked) step-up
lump-sum top-up only on a confirmed 10%+ index correction, from earmarked cash
long-only, unlevered accumulation
strategies.rationale: SIP / rupee-cost averaging works best in volatile, sideways and falling markets - exactly when manual investing stops - because its edge is behavioural, not mathematical (in a relentless bull run a single lump sum beats a SIP). Step-up SIPs align contributions with rising Indian incomes; value-averaging mechanically buys more units when prices are depressed and fewer when rich, lowering average cost per unit versus a flat SIP; and the 10%+ correction top-up leans into the drawdowns the SIP is designed to exploit. India note: the 12.5% LTCG rate above Rs 1.25 lakh rewards the long holding period, so the design avoids churn. This is the more honest accumulation benchmark; contrast the flat benchmark sip_baseline_buy_and_hold.
data_requirements: index drawdown-from-high series (for the 10%+ correction top-up trigger)

strategies.name: NIFTYBEES / GOLDBEES 2-Asset Allocation Scorecard
strategies.description: A two-asset portfolio construction study that blends a broad-equity ETF proxy (NIFTYBEES) with a gold ETF proxy (GOLDBEES) to study how a simple equity-plus-gold mix smooths the return path for Indian investors. Scorecard-only pending portfolio accounting and rebalance-cost modelling.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Studies static and lightly adaptive weight splits between the equity and gold ETF proxies (for example a fixed 70/30 sleeve and an inverse-volatility variant), evaluating periodic rebalancing back to target weights and the resulting risk-adjusted return profile.
strategies.exit: No positions. Target weights are restored at each rebalance study point; the study records how the split drifts between rebalances and how rebalancing frequency affects turnover and outcome.
filters: liquid, low-tracking-error ETF proxy per asset (equity, gold)
rebalance-band and rebalance-frequency awareness (turnover cost)
robust volatility estimation window for the adaptive variant
governance: two-asset scope only, no single-name concentration
long-only, unlevered
strategies.rationale: Gold has historically been a low-to-negative correlation diversifier to Indian equities and a rupee-depreciation and crisis hedge; studying a disciplined equity-plus-gold split quantifies how much drawdown smoothing a very simple two-ETF mix can add before any multi-asset engine is committed to paper capital.

strategies.name: Momentum Factor Composite Scorecard (12-1, Nifty 200)
strategies.description: Buy what has been strong - the best-documented factor premium in Indian equities. A single-factor scorecard that ranks the Nifty 200 by 12-1 month price momentum (twelve-month return skipping the most recent month) and holds the top 20-30 equal-weight, rebalanced monthly or quarterly. Notes the passive route: a Nifty 200 Momentum 30 index fund. Scorecard-only until portfolio accounting and rebalance-cost modelling are wired.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Computes each Nifty 200 name's trailing twelve-month return excluding the most recent month (12-1 momentum), normalises within sector, ranks the universe, and forms a top 20-30 equal-weight research basket studied on a monthly or quarterly reconstitution. Passive-route variant: track a Nifty 200 Momentum 30 index fund instead of a self-built basket.
strategies.exit: No positions. Names fall out of the study basket when their momentum rank decays below the inclusion threshold at the next reconstitution; the study logs the high turnover this implies and the tax/impact-cost drag it would incur if run as a self-built basket.
filters: Nifty 200 universe with liquidity floor for investability
sector-relative normalisation to avoid single-sector crowding
skip the most recent month (12-1) to reduce short-term reversal drag
monthly/quarterly reconstitution-turnover and cost awareness
regime awareness: momentum crashes hardest when regimes flip (e.g. March 2020) - see momentum_crash_guard_overlay_scorecard
long-only, unlevered basket study
strategies.rationale: Cross-sectional price momentum has historically been the highest-premium factor in Indian equities and works best in trending bull markets; skipping the most recent month sidesteps short-term reversal, and sector normalisation reduces the concentration and crash tendencies of raw momentum. The dominant risk is violent crash reversals when regimes flip, plus high-churn tax and impact costs. India note: the Nifty 200 Momentum 30 index-fund route converts a high-churn strategy into a single tax event on the investor's own exit, a genuine structural edge for individuals. Pairs with low_volatility_portfolio_scorecard and is de-risked by momentum_crash_guard_overlay_scorecard.

strategies.name: Low-Volatility Portfolio Scorecard
strategies.description: A portfolio construction study that ranks the liquid NSE universe by trailing realised volatility (and optionally beta) and builds a low-volatility research portfolio, studying inverse-volatility weighting versus equal weight. Scorecard-only pending portfolio accounting.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Estimates each name's trailing realised volatility over a rolling window, ranks the universe from calmest to most volatile, selects the lowest-volatility cohort subject to sector caps, and studies both equal-weight and inverse-volatility weighting of that cohort.
strategies.exit: No positions. Constituents and weights are recomputed at each rebalance study point as realised volatilities shift; the study records how membership and weights migrate and the turnover cost implied.
filters: liquidity floor for investability
robust realised-volatility estimation window
sector caps to prevent defensive-sector concentration
rebalance-cost and turnover awareness
long-only, unlevered
strategies.rationale: The low-volatility anomaly (calmer stocks delivering competitive returns at lower risk) is well documented in Indian equities and pairs naturally with the behavioural durability long-term investors need; studying the construction quantifies its drawdown and turnover profile before portfolio accounting is committed.

strategies.name: Value Factor Composite Scorecard
strategies.description: A single-factor scorecard that ranks the liquid NSE universe on a composite valuation tilt (earnings yield and book yield, i.e. inverse P/E and inverse P/B) to surface a research basket of statistically cheap names. Scorecard-only until fundamentals and portfolio accounting are wired.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Builds a blended value score from earnings yield (E/P) and book yield (B/P), optionally adding cash-flow yield, normalises the components within sector, ranks the universe, and forms a cheap-cohort research basket studied on a quarterly reconstitution cadence.
strategies.exit: No positions. Names leave the study basket when their composite value score re-rates above the inclusion threshold at the next reconstitution; the study logs the resulting turnover and any value-trap flags.
filters: sufficient fundamental history for stable ratios
exclude extreme-leverage / governance-flag names (value-trap guard)
liquidity floor for investability
sector-relative normalisation of valuation ratios
long-only, unlevered basket study
strategies.rationale: A composite value tilt diversifies across correlated cheapness signals rather than relying on a single ratio, reducing the accounting noise and value-trap risk of any one metric; the earnings/book-yield blend has a long history as a return premium in Indian equities when combined with a leverage and governance screen.

strategies.name: Momentum Crash-Guard Overlay Scorecard
strategies.description: A portfolio overlay study that de-risks a momentum basket during the bear-market rebound regimes where momentum historically crashes, scaling exposure by the market regime and by the recent volatility of the momentum strategy itself. Scorecard-only overlay.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Studies a rule that scales a momentum sleeve's exposure down when the broad market is in a drawdown/bear regime (e.g. index below its long-term average with elevated realised volatility) and up as the regime normalises, using a volatility-scaled target so exposure falls as the sleeve's own volatility spikes.
strategies.exit: No positions of its own. The overlay continuously adjusts the momentum sleeve's target weight at each study point; it holds no independent book and emits no directional signal.
filters: broad-market regime classifier (trend + realised volatility)
volatility-scaled exposure target for the momentum sleeve
avoid excessive de-risk/re-risk turnover (hysteresis band)
governance: overlay only, never a standalone signal
long-only, unlevered
strategies.rationale: Momentum's rare but severe crashes cluster in panicky bear-market rebounds when past losers snap back hardest; conditioning exposure on the market regime and on the strategy's own volatility has historically defanged those tail losses, so studying the overlay quantifies how much crash protection it adds before portfolio accounting is committed.

strategies.name: Rebalance-Bands / Tax-Loss-Harvest Overlay Scorecard
strategies.description: A portfolio maintenance overlay study that compares calendar rebalancing against tolerance-band rebalancing and layers a tax-loss-harvesting study, quantifying the trade-off between tracking a target allocation, turnover cost, and after-tax outcomes for Indian investors. Scorecard-only overlay.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Studies when to rebalance a target allocation using tolerance bands (act only when a sleeve drifts beyond a set percentage of its target) versus fixed calendar dates, and separately studies harvesting unrealised losses to offset gains while respecting a wash-sale-style re-entry gap and India's short/long-term capital-gains distinction.
strategies.exit: No positions. Weights are nudged back toward target only when a band is breached (or a calendar date arrives) at each study point; harvesting actions are recorded hypothetically with their modelled tax effect, never as live trades.
filters: tolerance-band width vs calendar-cadence comparison
turnover and transaction-cost awareness (STT/brokerage drag)
wash-sale-style re-entry gap to keep harvesting legitimate
respect short-term vs long-term capital-gains treatment
governance: overlay only, long-only, unlevered
strategies.rationale: Band-based rebalancing typically controls allocation drift at materially lower turnover than rigid calendar rebalancing, and disciplined tax-loss harvesting can add measurable after-tax return; studying both as an overlay quantifies the cost-versus-control and after-tax trade-offs before any live portfolio maintenance is wired.

strategies.name: Passive Index Core (Nifty 50 / Next 50 / Nifty 500)
strategies.description: The default equity core: own the market cheaply through broad index funds/ETFs so costs and taxes stay near zero. Blends Nifty 50 for large-cap beta with Nifty Next 50 and/or a Nifty 500 fund for breadth. This is the benchmark every other idea in Chapter 3 must beat after costs and taxes. Scorecard-only as a multi-vehicle portfolio study pending portfolio accounting and tracking-error/cost modelling; the single-vehicle executable analogue is sip_baseline_buy_and_hold.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Studies allocating the equity core across broad-market index vehicles - Nifty 50 for large-cap beta, Nifty Next 50 or a Nifty 500 fund for breadth - preferring direct-plan index funds with total expense ratios under 0.25% and low tracking error. Review nothing except an annual rebalance back to target index weights.
strategies.exit: No positions. Full-equity drawdowns are expected and are addressed by asset allocation (3.3, strategic_asset_allocation_rebalance_scorecard), not by exiting the core; the study records how a once-a-year rebalance keeps the core on its target index mix.
filters: direct-plan index funds / ETFs with total expense ratio under 0.25%
prefer low tracking error over headline name
ETF buyers: check on-exchange liquidity and iNAV spread before using market orders
annual (once-a-year) rebalance only; avoid over-trading the core
long-only, unlevered core
strategies.rationale: In India the empirical hierarchy is blunt: a low-cost index core beats most active effort after tax, so it is the honest benchmark for the whole investing book. The India-specific execution note is that a fund's realised edge is dominated by expense ratio and tracking error, and that ETF (rather than index-fund) buyers must respect on-exchange liquidity and iNAV spreads or a careless market order gives back the cost saving. Complements the single-ETF executable baseline sip_baseline_buy_and_hold and the multi-asset wrapper strategic_asset_allocation_rebalance_scorecard.
data_requirements: daily NAV / OHLCV for index funds and ETFs (Nifty 50, Nifty Next 50, Nifty 500)
ETF on-exchange liquidity and iNAV/bid-ask spread

strategies.name: Strategic Asset Allocation 60/30/10 with Calendar+Band Rebalance
strategies.description: The only free lunch: pre-committed selling high and buying low across assets. Fixes target weights across equity, debt and gold (illustratively 60% equity / 30% debt / 10% gold via index-fund/ETF proxies) and rebalances mechanically on a calendar (yearly) and/or a tolerance band (any sleeve drifts 5 percentage points). Scorecard-only as a multi-asset portfolio study pending portfolio accounting and rebalance-cost modelling.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Studies a fixed strategic weight mix across equity, debt/gilt and gold ETF or index-fund proxies (e.g. 60/30/10) and mechanically restores it either on a yearly calendar or whenever a sleeve drifts beyond a 5-percentage-point tolerance band, whichever the variant specifies. Execution is rule-based, never discretionary.
strategies.exit: No positions. The mix is trimmed and topped up back to target at each rebalance study point - which forces equity buying in crashes and trimming in euphoria across a full cycle; the study records turnover, tax/exit-load friction and how band width versus calendar cadence changes the outcome.
filters: liquid low-tracking-error ETF / index-fund proxy per asset (equity, debt/gilt, gold)
calendar-cadence vs tolerance-band (5pp) rebalance comparison
avoid bands too tight (tax and exit-load friction)
guard against rebalancing into a structurally dying asset
long-only, unlevered
strategies.rationale: Rebalancing a fixed multi-asset mix is the closest thing to a free lunch: it mechanically forces buying assets that have fallen and trimming those that have surged, working best across full cycles. Risks are friction (bands set too tight incur tax and exit-load cost) and mechanically topping up a structurally dying asset. India note: multi-asset or balanced-advantage funds outsource this with better tax treatment than manual switching, because their internal rebalancing is not a taxable event for the investor. Companion studies: the two-asset niftybees_goldbees_allocation_scorecard, the equal-risk risk_parity_allocation, and the band/tax mechanics in rebalance_bands_tax_harvest_overlay_scorecard.
data_requirements: multi-asset ETF / index-fund history (equity, debt/gilt, gold)
target weights and drift series per sleeve

strategies.name: Graham-Style Value Screen (Margin of Safety)
strategies.description: Buy a rupee of assets or earnings for sixty paise, diversified enough to survive being early. A Graham-style deep-value screen on hard accounting ratios with an explicit margin of safety, holding an equal-weight basket of 15-25 names reviewed annually. Scorecard-only: it needs fundamentals data this system does not ingest and multi-name portfolio accounting, so it emits no paper trades. Distinct from the statistical value tilt in value_factor_composite_scorecard by using absolute Graham thresholds and a governance-first filter rather than cross-sectional cheapness ranks.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Screens for P/E below 12-14, price-to-book below 1.5, debt-to-equity below 0.5, positive operating cash flow and a dividend record, then requires a margin of safety (price well below conservative intrinsic value). Builds an equal-weight research basket of roughly 15-25 names, reviewed annually.
strategies.exit: No positions. A name leaves the study basket on a thesis break or when it re-rates - the doctrine sells on 50-100% gains or when the value case is invalidated; the study logs realised re-rating and any value-trap flags.
filters: governance FIRST: screen out serial equity diluters, high promoter pledging and auditor churn before touching price ratios
P/E below 12-14
price-to-book below 1.5
debt-to-equity below 0.5
positive operating cash flow and a dividend record
margin of safety versus conservative intrinsic value
diversify across 15-25 names, equal weight (survive being early)
liquidity floor for investability
strategies.rationale: Deep value works best in post-crash markets and unloved sectors - Indian PSUs in 2020-21 were a textbook case. The dominant risk is the value trap: cheap because the business is dying, and in India governance discounts are often deserved. Hence the India note that governance (dilution, promoter pledging, auditor churn) is the FIRST filter here, not the last, and diversification across many names is what lets the investor survive being early. Cross-reference the quality lens quality_coffee_can_scorecard, the growth-disciplined garp_reasonable_growth_scorecard, and the ranked value_factor_composite_scorecard.

strategies.name: Quality / Coffee-Can Compounding
strategies.description: Buy dominant, high-return businesses and then do the hardest thing: nothing. A coffee-can quality screen filtering for durable, high-return-on-capital compounders held for a decade, acting only on business deterioration and ignoring valuation wobbles. Scorecard-only: needs a decade of fundamentals this system does not ingest plus multi-name portfolio accounting, so it emits no paper trades. Distinct from the blended quality_value_growth_composite by demanding a ten-year track record and near-zero debt and by its buy-and-forget hold.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Filters for ROCE and ROE above 18-20% sustained over ten years, revenue growth above 10%, negligible debt and honest capital allocation. Builds a concentrated 10-15 name research basket to hold a decade.
strategies.exit: No positions. A name is removed only on genuine business deterioration (return-on-capital erosion, leverage creep, capital-allocation red flags) - valuation swings are explicitly ignored; the study records deterioration triggers rather than price-based exits.
filters: ROCE and ROE above 18-20% sustained for ten years
revenue growth above 10%
negligible debt
honest, consistent capital allocation
concentrated 10-15 names, decade-long hold
act only on business deterioration, not valuation wobbles
liquidity floor for investability
strategies.rationale: Quality compounds quietly with shallower drawdowns, which suits investors who cannot watch markets daily. The central risk is paying any price - quality bought at 90x earnings spent 2022-23 going sideways while earnings caught up. India note: the long runway in financialisation and consumption is the thesis, and a GARP-style entry discipline (garp_reasonable_growth_scorecard) guards the multiple on the way in. Cross-reference the Graham deep-value counterpart graham_value_screen_scorecard.

strategies.name: GARP - Growth at a Reasonable Price
strategies.description: Growth investing with an adult supervising the multiple. A GARP screen pairing 15%+ earnings growth with a PEG discipline (P/E divided by growth below 1-1.2), improving margins and low leverage, entering on consolidations and exiting when the multiple stretches or growth decelerates. Scorecard-only: needs forward-growth and fundamentals data this system does not ingest plus multi-name portfolio accounting, so it emits no paper trades. Bridges graham_value_screen_scorecard and quality_coffee_can_scorecard by valuing growth without overpaying.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Screens for earnings growth of 15%+ with PEG (P/E divided by growth) below 1-1.2, improving margins and low leverage, and studies entries on price consolidations rather than chasing extended moves.
strategies.exit: No positions. The doctrine exits when PEG stretches beyond roughly 2 or when growth decelerates two quarters running; the study records these exit triggers and cross-checks that reported growth is backed by operating cash flow.
filters: earnings growth 15%+
PEG (P/E / growth) below 1-1.2 on entry
improving margins
low leverage
enter on consolidations, not extended moves
cross-check growth against operating-cash-flow conversion
exit when PEG > ~2 or growth decelerates two quarters running
liquidity floor for investability
strategies.rationale: GARP works best on mid-caps graduating to large-caps in expansionary phases of the cycle. The core risk is forecast error - the G in GARP is an estimate, and Indian mid-cap guidance is optimistic by habit - so the India note insists on cross-checking growth against cash-flow conversion, since profit growth without operating-cash-flow growth is an accounting story, not a business one. Cross-reference the value anchor graham_value_screen_scorecard and the quality-hold quality_coffee_can_scorecard.

strategies.name: Dividend-Yield Income Screen
strategies.description: Get paid to wait, in businesses that can afford the cheque. A dividend-yield income screen for sustainably high yielders with a payout cushion and a healthy balance sheet, reinvesting payouts and exiting on dividend cuts. Scorecard-only: needs dividend, payout and balance-sheet fundamentals this system does not ingest plus multi-name portfolio accounting, so it emits no paper trades. Serves as a lower-volatility income sleeve alongside the factor studies.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Screens for dividend yield above 3-4% with a payout ratio under 60%, stable earnings and net cash or low debt. Utilities, PSUs and mature IT typically dominate the screen; payouts are reinvested to compound.
strategies.exit: No positions. A name leaves the study basket on a dividend cut; the study also flags yield traps where the price has crashed ahead of the dividend and records how PSU payout cycles behave.
filters: dividend yield above 3-4%
payout ratio under 60% (cushion)
stable earnings
net cash or low debt
yield-trap guard: distinguish a high yield from a crashed price
PSU caveat: payouts follow government cash needs, not shareholder logic
liquidity floor for investability
strategies.rationale: Dividend-yield investing works best in sideways and high-rate environments and as a lower-volatility income sleeve. Risks are yield traps (price has crashed ahead of the dividend) and PSU payouts that track government cash needs rather than shareholder logic. India tax note: dividends are taxed at slab rates, so for high-bracket investors buyback-prone and low-payout compounders are often more tax-efficient than headline yield - a reason to weigh total shareholder return, not just the coupon. Cross-reference the quality lens quality_coffee_can_scorecard.

strategies.name: Magic Formula (Greenblatt, India Edition)
strategies.description: Rank the market on cheap plus good, buy the intersection, repeat annually. Greenblatt's Magic Formula adapted for India: rank the investable universe on earnings yield (EBIT/EV) and return on capital, sum the ranks, buy the top 20-30 equal-weight, and replace annually, excluding financials where EV metrics mislead. Scorecard-only: needs fundamentals this system does not ingest plus multi-name portfolio accounting, so it emits no paper trades.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Ranks all stocks above roughly Rs 500 crore market cap on earnings yield (EBIT/EV) and separately on return on capital, sums the two ranks, and forms an equal-weight research basket of the top 20-30 names, replaced annually. Financials are excluded because enterprise-value metrics mislead for them.
strategies.exit: No positions. The basket is fully reconstituted once a year on the combined rank; a name simply drops out if it no longer ranks in the top cohort. The study records the multi-year underperformance stretches that are the price of admission.
filters: market cap above roughly Rs 500 crore (liquidity/investability floor)
exclude financials (EV metrics mislead)
rank on earnings yield (EBIT/EV) and return on capital, sum ranks
buy top 20-30 equal-weight, replace annually
ADD a governance and promoter-pledging filter on top of the ranks
accept multi-year underperformance as the cost of the edge
strategies.rationale: The Magic Formula is a disciplined value-quality hybrid for investors who want rules, not stories: it mechanically buys businesses that are simultaneously cheap (high earnings yield) and good (high return on capital). The main risk is multi-year stretches of underperformance - the price of admission that makes most people quit at the worst time. India note: layer a governance and pledging filter on top of the raw ranks, because the formula cannot read footnotes and Indian small-caps punish those who don't. Cross-reference graham_value_screen_scorecard (value) and quality_coffee_can_scorecard (quality).

strategies.name: CANSLIM Growth-Momentum Hybrid (O'Neil)
strategies.description: Fundamental rocket fuel plus technical ignition, with a hard 7-8% stop. O'Neil's CANSLIM growth-momentum hybrid: current and annual EPS growth above 25%, a genuinely new driver, tight supply, leadership in a leading industry, institutional accumulation and a confirmed market uptrend, buying breakouts from sound bases and cutting every position at -7-8%. Scorecard-only: it fuses fundamentals, institutional-ownership and event data this system does not ingest with multi-name portfolio accounting, so it emits no paper trades.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Studies the CANSLIM checklist - C: current quarterly EPS growth above 25%; A: annual EPS growth above 25%; N: a genuinely new driver (product, management or cycle) and a new price high from a sound base; S: tight supply (small/absorbed float, buybacks); L: leadership in a leading industry (relative-strength leader); I: institutional accumulation; M: a confirmed market uptrend. Buys breakouts from sound bases only when M is green.
strategies.exit: No positions. The system's defining rule is a hard stop: cut every position at -7 to -8% without discussion. The study also exits on failed breakouts or when the market-direction (M) filter turns down, and records how strictly honouring the loss cap drives the outcome.
filters: C - current quarterly EPS growth above 25%
A - annual EPS growth above 25%
N - a genuinely new driver plus a new high from a sound base
S - tight supply (small/absorbed float, buybacks)
L - leadership in a leading industry (relative-strength leader)
I - institutional accumulation (rising fund ownership)
M - confirmed market uptrend: Nifty above its 50- and 200-day averages
hard 7-8% stop on every position, no exceptions
strategies.rationale: CANSLIM works best in early and mid bull markets with broad participation. Whipsaw losses cluster in choppy tapes, and the system's real edge is the loss cap - which most users abandon first. India note: the M (market direction) filter maps cleanly to the Nifty trading above its 50- and 200-day averages, so the strategy is only traded when the tide is in; this connects to golden_cross_index_timing and dma_momentum_50_200 for the regime gate. Overlaps the swing relative-strength leader idea but sits in the investment desk as a positional scorecard.
data_requirements: quarterly and annual EPS-growth history (C, A)
Nifty 50-day and 200-day moving averages for market direction (M)

strategies.name: Special Situations Event Basket
strategies.description: Return streams driven by corporate events, not market direction. A special-situations basket across buybacks (tender-offer arbitrage on acceptance ratios), demergers/spin-offs, open offers, delisting attempts, promoter-buying clusters and index-inclusion flows, sized small per situation. Scorecard-only: it is entirely dependent on corporate-action and event data this system does not ingest, so it emits no paper trades. The edge is low correlation to beta.
strategies.entry: No paper trades emitted. Studies a diversified basket of event-driven situations: buyback tender-offer arbitrage (built on acceptance-ratio maths), demergers and spin-offs (systematically mispriced), open offers, delisting attempts, clusters of promoter buying and index-inclusion flows. Each situation is sized small; the edge is the repeatable process, not any single deal.
strategies.exit: No positions. Each situation resolves on its own corporate-event timeline (tender acceptance, record/listing date, offer close, delisting outcome, index effective date); the study records deal outcomes, breaks and realised acceptance ratios rather than price-based exits.
filters: diversify across event types; size each situation small
buyback: model the acceptance ratio from the offer document's ratio history, not optimistically
watch deal-break and regulatory-delay risk
SEBI tender-route buybacks reserve up to Rs 2 lakh holdings for small shareholders (retail edge)
event/corporate-action data dependency
low correlation to market beta is the objective
strategies.rationale: Special situations work in any market because the point is low correlation to beta - returns come from corporate events, not index direction. The risks are deal breaks, regulatory delay and acceptance-ratio maths done optimistically. India note: SEBI's tender-route buyback framework and its generous small-shareholder reservation (up to Rs 2 lakh holdings) have historically favoured retail participants, but each offer document's ratio history must be read before assuming a favourable acceptance ratio. Complementary event study: the swing-desk post_earnings_drift_scorecard_equity for results-driven moves.
data_requirements: corporate-action / event calendar (buybacks, demergers, open offers, delistings)
buyback offer documents and historical acceptance ratios
index-inclusion / rebalance announcements and flow estimates

strategies.name: Moving-Average Crossover Trend System (Equity)
strategies.description: A fully mechanical, low-frequency trend harvester on liquid NSE names: goes long on a moving-average golden cross and stays until the reverse cross or a trail under the slower average. It never top-ticks and never misses a big move; a 40-50% win rate is carried by 2.5R+ average winners.
strategies.entry: Long when the 20-EMA crosses above the 50-EMA for a swing timeframe (or the 50-DMA crosses above the 200-DMA for a positional hold), taken on the confirmed daily close of the cross with no discretion. Prefer names above their rising 200-DMA and with the Nifty regime not in a sharp downtrend so the crossover is with the higher-timeframe tide.
strategies.exit: Primary exit is the reverse cross (fast average back below the slow average) on a confirmed close. Alternatively trail a stop under the slower average and exit on a decisive close beneath it. Once the position reaches roughly +0.8R move the stop to breakeven and thereafter ratchet it up under the slower average, locking about 60% of the maximum favourable excursion so trends convert into multi-R winners instead of round trips. No overrides, no early profit-taking on discretion.
filters: F&O-liquid or index constituent only
trade only when frequency stays low (avoid over-fitting to choppy tapes)
prefer price above rising 200-DMA for the higher-timeframe tide
expect and accept 40-50% win rate with 2.5R+ winners
avoid names with imminent earnings on the entry day
Nifty regime not in a sharp downtrend
strategies.rationale: A dumb, robust, honest system: it will never top-tick and never miss a big move, and its edge lives entirely in a few large winners that pay for many small whipsaw losses. Backtests on Nifty and liquid F&O names hold up after costs precisely because trade frequency is low, so the STT/brokerage drag per unit of return stays small. The R-ratchet is the highest-leverage upgrade because the historical flaw of crossover systems is capturing only a fraction of the move.

strategies.name: Supertrend / ADX Trend Following (Equity)
strategies.description: Volatility-adjusted trend following on liquid, high-beta NSE names: goes long when price closes above Supertrend(10,3) with ADX confirming trend strength, uses the Supertrend line itself as the trailing stop, and stands aside when ADX shows no trend. Because the ATR-based stop widens with volatility, position size is always derived from the current stop distance.
strategies.entry: Long when the daily close flips above Supertrend(10,3) AND ADX(14) is above 20-25 (rising ADX preferred), confirming a real trend rather than range chop. Stand aside entirely when ADX is below 20. Prefer names above the rising 50-DMA with the Nifty regime supportive. Size the position by dividing the per-trade rupee risk by the current close-to-Supertrend distance, so the ATR-widened stop never lets rupee risk quietly double.
strategies.exit: The Supertrend(10,3) line is the trailing stop: exit on a confirmed daily close back below Supertrend (the flip to a down regime). Optionally exit sooner if ADX collapses back under 20 (trend energy gone). Once the trade reaches roughly +0.8R the effective stop is already at or beyond breakeven via the rising Supertrend; let the one-way Supertrend ratchet lock the move. Do not widen the stop when ATR expands; if the recomputed stop distance would breach the rupee cap, reduce size rather than loosen the stop.
filters: F&O-liquid or index constituent only
ADX(14) above 20-25 to confirm trend; stand aside if below 20
price closed above Supertrend(10,3)
prefer price above rising 50-DMA and supportive Nifty regime
recompute size from the CURRENT stop distance, never a fixed quantity
avoid names with imminent earnings
avoid circuit-bound names
strategies.rationale: Supertrend adapts stop distance to each instrument's volatility, and the ADX gate removes most of the range-chop losers that plague trend systems. It is a staple on high-beta Indian names and index-linked stocks. The critical discipline is sizing: because ATR-based stops widen in volatility, quantity must be an output of the current stop distance, otherwise the rupee risk silently doubles when volatility spikes.

strategies.name: Bollinger Squeeze Expansion Breakout (Equity)
strategies.description: Trades the expansion break out of a Bollinger-band squeeze in liquid NSE names: when band width compresses to a multi-month low, volatility is coiled; the strategy waits for the expansion break and trades its direction (long only), using the opposite band as the stop. Regime classification comes first - this is the squeeze playbook, not the reversion one.
strategies.entry: First classify the regime: require Bollinger(20,2) band width at or near a 6-month low (a genuine volatility squeeze / contraction). Then wait for the expansion break - a decisive close outside the upper band on expanding range and volume. Take the long only when the break is UP (higher-timeframe trend and market regime supportive); do not fade the squeeze. Squeezes ahead of results and policy events are the highest-quality setups but require the event itself to be tradable (no position held blind into the print).
strategies.exit: Stop at the opposite (lower) Bollinger band, or below the squeeze base low, whichever keeps 1R inside the per-trade rupee cap. Target the measured expansion (band-width normalisation / prior swing objective); book a partial into the first target, move to breakeven near +0.8R, then ratchet the stop up under successive higher lows or the rising middle band, locking about 60% of MFE. Exit if price closes back inside the squeeze range (failed expansion).
filters: F&O-liquid or index constituent only
REGIME CHECK FIRST: band width at a ~6-month low (squeeze confirmed)
trade only the expansion break, long only on an upside break
break confirmed by range/volume expansion
do not apply the reversion playbook on a squeeze day (modes are mutually exclusive)
if event-driven (results/policy), the event must be tradable, not held blind
avoid circuit-bound names
strategies.rationale: Volatility contraction precedes expansion; a multi-month band-width low marks coiled energy that resolves into a directional move. Trading the direction of the break - not a prediction of it - with the opposite band as a natural stop captures the expansion leg. On Indian index-linked and high-beta names, squeeze breaks around policy events are a recurring, tradeable pattern. The cardinal error is using the mean-reversion playbook on a squeeze day, so regime classification is the first gate.

strategies.name: Bollinger Band Mean Reversion (Equity)
strategies.description: Fades stretched closes back to the 20-day mean in FLAT, sideways liquid large-caps: when a stock in a non-trending regime closes outside the lower Bollinger band, it buys the snap-back toward the 20-day middle band (long only). Regime classification comes first - this is the reversion playbook, applied only when there is no squeeze and no trend.
strategies.entry: First classify the regime as flat / range-bound (a sideways Bollinger channel, no active squeeze, no strong directional trend). Then buy when price closes outside the LOWER ±2 sigma band and prints a reversal candle back inside the band; require the name to be a liquid large-cap in a genuine range, with the Nifty regime not in a sharp downtrend. Long only (fading the upper band would require a short and is out of scope for cash equity).
strategies.exit: Primary target is the 20-day middle band (the mean); book the bulk there. Stop below the reversal-candle low / recent extreme, sized to the per-trade rupee cap. If the mean is reached quickly, optionally trail a remainder toward the upper band. Hard invalidation: a decisive expansion close that trends away from the mean (the range has broken, so the reversion thesis is void) - exit immediately rather than average down.
filters: F&O-liquid large-cap in a genuine flat / range regime
REGIME CHECK FIRST: no active squeeze, no strong trend
close outside the lower ±2 sigma band plus a reversal candle
do not apply the reversion playbook on a squeeze / expansion day
abandon if the band expands and price trends away from the mean
avoid names with imminent earnings
sufficient liquidity for a clean exit at the mean
strategies.rationale: In flat trends a stock oscillates around its 20-day mean, and closes outside the ±2 sigma band mark statistically stretched dislocations that tend to revert. Taking the long-only fade of the lower band toward the mean is a high-probability reversion in sideways large-caps. The two Bollinger modes - squeeze breakout and band reversion - are mutually exclusive, so classifying the regime as range-bound before deploying this playbook is essential; using it on a squeeze/expansion day is the documented failure mode.
data_requirements: Bollinger Bands (20,2) and middle band (20-day mean)

strategies.name: Classical Chart Pattern Scorecard
strategies.description: A manual-review / backtest-only study of classical chart patterns on liquid NSE names. It scores continuation patterns (flags, pennants, cup-and-handle) as trend-direction breakout setups with measured-move targets and pattern-internal stops, and treats reversal patterns (head-and-shoulders, double tops) as exit signals rather than fresh cash shorts. No orders are emitted; every pattern's invalidation is predefined before scoring.
strategies.entry: Study-only: identifies continuation patterns in the prevailing trend (flag/pennant off a strong pole, cup-and-handle) and records the theoretical long trigger as the breakout of the pattern boundary on above-average volume, only in liquid names enough participants watch for the structure to be self-fulfilling. For each candidate it predefines the invalidation level BEFORE scoring (if it needs squinting, it is not there). Reversal patterns (head-and-shoulders, double top) are logged as exit / de-risk signals for existing longs, not as short entries. No orders emitted.
strategies.exit: Study-only: theoretical targets are measured moves (flag: pole height projected from the breakout; cup-and-handle: cup depth projected upward), with a pattern-internal stop (e.g. below the flag low or handle low). Outcomes are tracked to characterise hit rate, measured-move attainment, and false-break frequency. No positions managed.
filters: liquid, widely watched names only (self-fulfilling structure)
continuation patterns scored in the trend direction only
breakout must be confirmed by volume expansion
predefine pattern invalidation before scoring (no pattern hallucination)
reversal patterns treated as exit signals, not fresh cash shorts
India note: positional shorts on reversal patterns require futures or puts (own margin / expiry maths); cash shorts are intraday-only
flag confounding corporate actions and earnings
strategies.rationale: Flags, pennants, cup-and-handle and head-and-shoulders are structured supply-demand footprints with measurable targets, and in liquid names they become partly self-fulfilling because many participants act on the same structure. Because pattern recognition is subjective and prone to hallucination, and because shorting reversal patterns in the Indian cash market is intraday-only (positional shorts need futures/puts), this remains a manual-review / backtest-ready scorecard that predefines invalidation and never emits live orders.

strategies.name: Intraday Pullback Continuation (Retest Reclaim, Brooks)
strategies.description: The retest entry for any confirmed intraday breakout on a liquid NSE name: same breakout idea, roughly half the stop, double the R. Instead of chasing the break, it waits for price to pull back and retest the broken level (or the 20-EMA on the 5-minute), enters on the reclaim candle, and stops just beyond the retest low. It skips the 11:30-13:15 lunch chop.
strategies.entry: After a confirmed intraday breakout (a decisive 5-minute close beyond a key level with volume), do NOT chase. Wait for an orderly two-legged pullback that retests the broken level from above - or holds the rising 20-EMA on the 5-minute - and enter long on the reclaim candle (a bullish bar that closes back above the broken level / prior minor swing high). Require price on the correct side of VWAP and the confirming index not fighting the move; entry window 09:45-15:00 but skip entries during the 11:30-13:15 lunch chop.
strategies.exit: Stop just beyond (a few ticks below) the retest swing low, which is far tighter than a chase stop and roughly doubles the R-multiple. Book a partial at +1R, move to breakeven near +0.8R, then ratchet-trail the runner under 5-minute higher-lows (or the rising 20-EMA), locking about 60% of the maximum favourable excursion; time exit 14:45, force exit 15:20 IST. Accept that strong moves which never pull back are missed trades - the tight stop is the edge being bought.
filters: F&O-liquid or index constituent only
confirmed prior intraday breakout of a key level on volume
retest holds the broken level or the 20-EMA (5-min); reclaim candle required
price on the correct side of VWAP; confirming index not fighting
skip entries during the 11:30-13:15 lunch chop
skip earnings/result days and circuit-bound names
strategies.rationale: Chasing a breakout puts the stop far below entry and routinely exceeds a small account's per-trade rupee cap; the retest of the broken level keeps 1R inside a fixed rupee budget while targeting the identical move, so the pullback entry roughly doubles the R-multiple (Figure 11). Demanding a reclaim candle avoids catching a failed break, and skipping the lunch-window chop removes the lowest-quality intraday tape.

strategies.name: Range Fade at Support/Resistance (Equity)
strategies.description: On confirmed intraday range days, buys the floor of an established support/resistance range in a liquid NSE name: it fades the third-plus touch of support on a rejection candle, stops just beyond the range extreme, and targets the mid or opposite edge. Small targets, smaller egos - it abandons the playbook the moment a side breaks on volume, and skips index targets thinner than the friction floor.
strategies.entry: Qualify a range day first: at least two touches on EACH side of a horizontal range plus a wide CPR (range-day bias). Take the long only at the SUPPORT floor, on the third-or-later touch, when a bullish rejection candle prints; require the confirming index also range-bound; entry window 09:45-15:00 but skip the 11:30-13:15 chop. Do not initiate a fresh fade in the last 15 minutes.
strategies.exit: Stop just beyond the range extreme (below support), sized to the per-trade rupee cap; primary target the range mid, optionally trailing a runner toward the opposite (resistance) edge; book the bulk at the mid. Abandon the playbook immediately - flatten - the moment support breaks on volume (the breakout you are fading is the day's real trade). Time exit 14:45, force exit 15:20 IST.
filters: F&O-liquid or index constituent only
confirmed range day: two touches each side + wide CPR
fade only the third-plus touch of support on a rejection candle (long only)
abandon on a volume break of the range
skip the 11:30-13:15 chop and fresh entries in the last 15 minutes
friction floor: skip when the mid/edge target is thinner than ~0.25% on the index (costs eat small targets)
avoid circuit-bound names
strategies.rationale: On confirmed range days (expiry-adjacent drift, pre-event coiling) price oscillates between two-sided support and resistance, and the third-plus touch of a boundary tends to reject; fading support toward the mid on a rejection candle with a stop just beyond the extreme is a defined, long-only edge. The discipline is to abandon the fade the instant a side breaks on volume - that break is the day's real trade - and to skip targets smaller than the ~0.25% friction floor, below which the Indian cost stack eats the edge.

strategies.name: Index Long Call / Long Put Directional
strategies.description: Maximum-convexity defined-risk directional buy: a single near-the-money index CE or PE (NIFTY or BANKNIFTY) taken on a confirmed momentum/breakout trigger. Loss is capped at the premium paid; the strict rupee premium cap is the entire risk control. Index-only because single-stock option spreads are far too wide to execute realistically.
strategies.entry: After 09:45, on a 5-minute close beyond the opening range / prior-day pivot in the trend direction with a volume proxy >= 1.5x and price on the breakout side of VWAP: buy a single 0.40-0.60 delta (near-the-money) index option in the trade direction. Total premium x lot must fit under the max_trade_loss cap. Stay near the money — deep-OTM lottery buying is the retail loss engine.
strategies.exit: Premium stop at -40% to -50% of the premium paid, or on the underlying structure stop (5-minute close back inside the trigger); take profits into strength (book 50% at +1R, trail the remainder) because theta punishes patience; time exit 14:45, force exit 15:20 IST. Never hold a full loser to expiry.
filters: index underlying only (NIFTY / BANKNIFTY); never single-stock options
near-the-money strike only (0.40-0.60 delta); no deep-OTM lottery buys
avoid buying elevated IV ahead of a known binary event (IV-crush risk)
India VIX <= 24
full premium payable upfront; net premium x lot <= max_trade_loss cap
skip lunch-window (11:30-13:15) initiations and event-minute windows
strategies.rationale: A single long option gives open-ended payoff for a known, premium-capped worst case, which is exactly how a fixed rupee risk cap is enforced on a directional view. It shines on strong trends and expiry-day afternoons when gamma is explosive; the offsetting cost is theta and IV crush, so entries are gated on momentum confirmation and low/moderate IV, and profits are taken into strength. Restricted to index underlyings because single-stock option round-trip spreads (~15%) make single-stock long options non-executable.

strategies.name: Iron Butterfly Scorecard
strategies.description: Scorecard-only study of the iron butterfly: sell the ATM straddle (short ATM call + short ATM put) and buy symmetric protective wings, betting on a pin to the short strike at expiry. Net short premium on the ATM body: never executable. Distinct from the net-debit long_butterfly_scorecard, which buys the wings and shorts the body for a small debit.
strategies.entry: Study-only, no orders emitted. Log candidate iron butterflies centred on a strong expiry pin (heavy-OI / max-pain strike) in low-drift conditions: sell the ATM straddle, buy symmetric wings, collecting a credit. Tuesday Nifty expiry pinning around heavy-OI strikes is the natural habitat.
strategies.exit: Study-only, no positions managed. Record hypothetical management at 25-40% of credit rather than chasing the pin (small drift converts winners to scratches); note the tested-side adjustment when a short delta expands.
filters: expiry-day / expiry-week, low expected-move regime
strong max-pain / high-OI central strike
block on trend / expansion regime
+2% expiry-day ELM applies to the short legs
requires assignment / pin-risk and margin modelling before any consideration
strategies.rationale: An iron butterfly is a condor squeezed to a point: maximum credit but a very narrow profit zone, so discipline on the early take-profit is the whole game. The short ATM straddle body makes it a net-short-premium multi-leg structure, kept scorecard-only under the safety rails. Cross-reference long_butterfly_scorecard for the mirror-image net-debit pin bet.

strategies.name: Covered Call Income Scorecard
strategies.description: Scorecard-only study of the covered call: hold a delivered stock position and sell a monthly OTM call against it for income. Embedded short call (option selling) plus a full F&O lot of stock: never executable, and single-stock options are non-executable regardless.
strategies.entry: Study-only, no orders emitted. Against each delivered lot-equivalent of a quality holding, log a short monthly 3-7% OTM call (~0.2-0.3 delta) in a sideways-to-mildly-bullish phase; target 1-2% of stock value per month in premium.
strategies.exit: Study-only, no positions managed. Record hypothetical roll or assignment at expiry; note that a melt-up caps upside at the short strike and, in a crash, the premium is only a cosmetic cushion (this is income, not a hedge).
filters: single-stock options are never executable (~15% round-trip spreads)
quality holding you are happy to trim higher
sideways-to-mildly-bullish phase only
requires one full F&O lot of the underlying (Rs 2-6 lakh+ per liquid name)
index-proxy version needs Rs 16 lakh+ of basket
strategies.rationale: A covered call rents out upside you would sell anyway, but it caps the melt-up and does not protect the downside, and it demands portfolio-scale capital (a full lot per name). The short call is option selling, so it stays scorecard-only; single-stock options are additionally non-executable due to wide spreads.

strategies.name: Cash-Secured Put Scorecard
strategies.description: Scorecard-only study of the cash-secured put: sell monthly OTM puts on quality F&O names you would gladly own, with cash reserved for assignment. Embedded short put (option selling) on a multi-lakh single-stock commitment: never executable, and single-stock options are non-executable regardless. Feeds the wheel into covered_call_income_scorecard (6.8).
strategies.entry: Study-only, no orders emitted. On quality F&O names you actually want to own, log a short monthly 5-8% OTM put at strong support with cash reserved for assignment, preferably in elevated IV after a sentiment-driven drop.
strategies.exit: Study-only, no positions managed. Record keeping the premium if unexercised, or taking delivery if assigned and then switching to the covered-call study (6.8) - the wheel. Note assignment lands precisely when the name has broken down, so the ownership thesis must be real.
filters: single-stock options are never executable (~15% round-trip spreads)
quality F&O name you genuinely want to own
elevated IV after a sentiment-driven drop
cash (or margin) reserved for full assignment
each name is a multi-lakh commitment (Rs 15 lakh+ contract values)
strategies.rationale: A cash-secured put gets you paid to place a limit order at a price where you would gladly own the stock, and it is the entry leg of the wheel (6.9 into 6.8). But assignment risk concentrates exactly when the name breaks down, and the short put is option selling on a multi-lakh single-stock position, so it stays scorecard-only and non-executable.

strategies.name: Diagonal / Poor Man's Covered Call Scorecard
strategies.description: Scorecard-only study of the diagonal / poor man's covered call: buy a deep ITM (0.70-0.80 delta) far-month call as a stock substitute and sell near-dated OTM calls against it. Embedded short near leg (option selling) in a calendar structure: never executable.
strategies.entry: Study-only, no orders emitted. Log a long 0.70-0.80 delta far-month (1-3 month) call as the stock substitute, then a short near-dated OTM call against it, keeping the short strike above the long's breakeven, in a grinding uptrend on the index or a liquid large-cap.
strategies.exit: Study-only, no positions managed. Record rolling the short leg while holding the long as long as the trend lasts; note a sharp drop hits the long leg with little offset, so management is more active than a true covered call.
filters: grinding uptrend regime
short strike kept above the long call's breakeven
long leg 0.70-0.80 delta far-month; short leg near-dated OTM
embedded short near call requires margin / roll modelling
SEBI removed expiry-day calendar margin benefit - plan naked-leg margin
strategies.rationale: A PMCC drops the capital of a covered call from Rs 16 lakh+ (index basket) to roughly the deep-ITM premium (often Rs 40-90k on Nifty), making income-on-trend accessible to mid-sized accounts. But the recurring short near-dated call is option selling, and the calendar structure is outside the executable set, so it is retained scorecard-only.

strategies.name: Delta-Neutral / Gamma Scalping Scorecard
strategies.description: Scorecard-only study of gamma scalping: own a long ATM straddle (long gamma, long vega) and re-hedge delta to zero with index futures at set thresholds, profiting when realised volatility exceeds the implied volatility paid. It is LONG the straddle (not option selling), but the straddle structure plus the continuous futures-hedging requirement force it to be non-executable.
strategies.entry: Study-only, no orders emitted. Log a long ATM straddle when IV percentile is low but the tape whips (pre-regime-change compression), then log the futures hedges that re-flatten delta at set thresholds - each re-hedge mechanically sells high and buys low.
strategies.exit: Study-only, no positions managed. Record accumulated hedge P&L vs theta bleed and vega; the position is profitable only when realised vol beats the implied vol paid. Note Nifty futures lot granularity (65) makes hedges chunky.
filters: low IV percentile but whippy realised tape
requires continuous / automated delta re-hedging with futures
hedge friction and lot granularity (65 Nifty) modelled
theta bleed if realised vol underdelivers
most individuals better served by long straddle/strangle (6.10) fixed risk
strategies.rationale: Gamma scalping trades volatility itself: long the straddle and repeatedly re-hedge delta with futures, harvesting the wiggles when realised vol beats implied. It is a full-attention or fully-automated discipline with nothing in between, and the straddle structure combined with the live continuous-hedging requirement makes it non-executable in a paper-only, no-live-order system. Most individuals are better served expressing the same view via 6.10 (long straddle/strangle) with fixed risk.
data_requirements: index futures price and lot size

strategies.name: Positional Futures Trend (Scorecard)
strategies.description: Scorecard-only study of positional trend trading on NSE index (and highly liquid stock) futures, taking the Section 4 trend signals (4.3 moving-average crossover, 4.4 Supertrend/ADX) as the linear-leverage vehicle and riding the move with a volatility trail. Never executable: one index lot is ~Rs 16-18 lakh notional carrying undefined overnight-gap and mark-to-market risk, so it sits outside the defined-risk executable set.
strategies.entry: Study-only: log a candidate long/short of one index-future lot per signal when a Section 4 trend model fires (moving-average crossover per 4.3 or Supertrend/ADX trend confirmation per 4.4), sized to one lot per signal. No orders emitted.
strategies.exit: Study-only: record the hypothetical stop as a Supertrend flip or a 2.5-3 ATR volatility stop, ratchet-trailed as the trend extends; assume a roll of the position into the next month 1-2 days before expiry rather than holding through expiry. No positions managed.
filters: restrict to index futures and the most liquid stock futures (>= Rs 15 lakh contract value)
trade only sustained-trend regimes where a linear futures payoff beats a theta-bleeding option
flag overnight-gap exposure on ~Rs 16-18 lakh notional per lot
under the 1% rule a stop 1% away already risks Rs 16,000+ per lot, implying a Rs 10 lakh+ capital base
requires margin, roll, gap and slippage modelling before any execution consideration
strategies.rationale: In sustained trends a linear futures payoff outperforms a theta-bleeding long option, and Section 4 trend signals give the entry with a volatility trail managing the exit. It stays scorecard-only because index futures carry undefined, leveraged, overnight-gap risk (~Rs 16-18 lakh notional per lot, ~Rs 2-2.6 lakh margin) and mark-to-market calls in drawdowns, none of which fits the defined-risk executable invariant; effectively a Rs 10 lakh+ capital strategy under the 1% rule.
data_requirements: Section 4 trend signal inputs (moving averages, Supertrend, ADX, ATR)
exchange margin (SPAN + exposure) schedule per contract
lot size per contract (Nifty 65, BankNifty 30, FinNifty 60)
cost stack (index-futures brokerage, STT 0.02% sell, exchange, stamp, GST)

strategies.name: Cash-Futures Basis Arbitrage (Scorecard)
strategies.description: Scorecard-only study of cash-futures (basis) arbitrage: buy the underlying stock/index constituent in the cash market and short its future one-to-one to strip out equity risk and earn the carry when the futures premium is rich. Nearly market-neutral but never executable here: it needs two-leg cash+futures execution, futures margin and dividend/funding modelling, and only pays single-digit annualised returns that make sense at scale, not in a paper research engine.
strategies.entry: Study-only: log a candidate arbitrage when the futures premium (basis) annualises comfortably above a funding-plus-cost hurdle, buying cash and shorting the matching future one-to-one; note the dividend-adjusted basis around distributions. No orders emitted.
strategies.exit: Study-only: record the hypothetical unwind at basis convergence near expiry, or a roll into the next month when that month's basis still pays above the hurdle; flag forced early unwinds. No positions managed.
filters: annualised basis must clear the funding-plus-full-cost hurdle by a comfortable margin
favour bullish, leverage-hungry markets where futures trade rich, and modelled dividend distortions
flag leg-slippage between the cash buy and futures short
flag forced early unwind and single-digit-annualised return ceiling
arbitrage funds productise this with equity-fund taxation; personal use only makes sense at scale with cheap execution
strategies.rationale: Buying cash against a short future removes directional equity risk and pockets the carry when futures are rich, so the position is nearly market-neutral. It is kept scorecard-only because the edge is single-digit-annualised and decided by costs and execution: legs can slip, unwinds can be forced, and it requires two-market execution plus futures margin, funding and dividend modelling that no paper engine here provides. Institutional arbitrage funds run exactly this with favourable equity-fund taxation.
data_requirements: synchronised cash (spot) and futures quotes for each name
basis and annualised carry series per expiry
cost stack (delivery-equity STT 0.1% both sides, futures STT 0.02% sell, brokerage, exchange, stamp, GST)
futures margin (SPAN + exposure) and cash-leg capital requirement
dividend / corporate-action calendar for basis adjustment
lot size and minimum contract value per name

strategies.name: Futures Calendar Spread (Scorecard)
strategies.description: Scorecard-only study of FUTURES calendar (curve) spreads: long one expiry month and short another of the same underlying to trade the shape of the futures curve when the near-far spread deviates from its historical carry range. This is a futures-curve spread on the SAME underlying, distinct from the options calendar_spread_scorecard (which trades the option IV term structure across expiries at the same strike). Never executable: undefined leveraged spread risk plus loss of expiry-day spread-margin benefit under SEBI rules.
strategies.entry: Study-only: log a candidate spread when the near-far futures spread deviates from its historical carry range, buying the relatively cheap month and selling the rich month; direction is mostly hedged by holding both legs of the same underlying. No orders emitted.
strategies.exit: Study-only: record the hypothetical exit on spread normalisation back toward the carry range, or a close before near-month expiry (the SEBI rule removes the spread-margin benefit on expiry day). No positions managed.
filters: restrict to index futures and a handful of the most liquid stock futures; elsewhere the bid-ask is the whole edge
trade around roll windows when large players shift months and distort the near-far spread
account for the SEBI rule that removes spread-margin benefit on expiry day
low spread volatility makes position-sizing creep the real danger
requires the full multi-expiry margin and carry model before any execution consideration
strategies.rationale: The near-far futures spread reflects cost-of-carry; when large players roll positions around expiry windows it dislocates from its historical range and can be faded by going long the cheap month and short the rich one, with direction largely hedged. It remains scorecard-only because it is still a leveraged, undefined-risk futures position: the spread-margin benefit vanishes on expiry day under SEBI rules, and the low volatility of the spread tempts oversizing. Explicitly distinct from the options calendar spread, which trades IV term structure at one strike rather than the futures price curve.
data_requirements: multi-expiry futures curve (near, next, far) per underlying
calendar-spread margin rules incl. expiry-day removal (SEBI)
lot size and minimum contract value per underlying
cost stack per leg (futures brokerage, STT 0.02% sell, exchange, stamp, GST)
